source_title: Perceptive Humanoid Parkour: Chaining Dynamic Human Skills via Motion Matching

ai_summary.one_liner: PHP composes retargeted human parkour skills with motion matching and distills privileged RL experts into one depth-based Unitree G1 policy for autonomous long-horizon humanoid parkour.
ai_summary.problem: Humanoid parkour needs dynamic whole-body skills, perception-driven timing, and long-horizon skill transitions, but reward shaping alone struggles to discover arm-supported climbing and isolated human motion clips do not teach when and how to transition.
ai_summary.solution: Retarget atomic human parkour skills with OmniRetarget, synthesize obstacle-adaptive long-horizon references via motion matching, train privileged single-skill motion-tracking teachers, then distill them into a single depth-conditioned student with DAgger plus PPO.
ai_summary.key_result: On Unitree G1 hardware, PHP climbs a 1.25 m wall in 3.63 s, performs cat/speed vaults near 3 m/s, adapts to about 0.5 m real-time obstacle displacement, and in simulation reports 0.95-1.00 success across 36/58/76 cm obstacles at 1.0 m/s and 2.0 m/s.
limitations: The pipeline currently lacks semantic scene understanding and richer conditioning such as language.
Real-world capability is constrained by a short-range, narrow-field-of-view depth camera at high running speed, causing perceptual ambiguity when obstacles appear too late.
The hardware lacks sufficiently strong hands or grippers for edge/bar interactions, so beyond-height climbing and hanging maneuvers are not tested.

research_gaps.question: Can a humanoid parkour policy reason semantically about obstacle affordances instead of relying mainly on depth geometry and velocity commands?
How much of PHP's success transfers to robots with different hand strength, camera placement, field of view, and actuation limits?
Can motion matching and interaction-preserving retargeting be combined with VLA-style task conditioning for user-directed parkour routes and style control?

future_directions: Add semantic scene understanding or language conditioning so the robot can choose among routes and styles beyond discrete velocity commands.
Improve depth sensing range, field of view, and latency robustness for high-speed approach decisions.
Test stronger hands or grippers for edge, rail, and bar interactions beyond wall climbing.
Benchmark cross-embodiment transfer of motion-matching reference libraries across humanoid platforms.
